items.q: Why might a business adopt corporate social responsibility (CSR)?
items.correct: Reasons include genuine ethics, but also commercial benefits — improved reputation, customer loyalty, employee retention, and reduced regulatory or reputational risk.
items.misconception: CSR is purely about being ethical with no business benefit.
items.random_wrong: CSR is the legal requirement to pay taxes.
items.why: CSR is both ethical and strategic. Students often treat it as either pure morality or pure cynicism — in business studies the balanced answer recognises both motivations.

items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.q: What are the four Ps of the marketing mix?
items.correct: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion — the controllable variables a business uses to satisfy customers and achieve objectives.
items.misconception: The four Ps are Product, Price, Profit, and People.
items.random_wrong: The four Ps are Planning, Producing, Promoting, and Profit.
items.why: Students often substitute 'profit' or 'people' into the four Ps. The classic McCarthy framework is Product, Price, Place and Promotion — extended models add others but those four are foundational.
It's Where buyers and sellers meet. “Just a town square” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Left-handed scissors are an example of a…
items.why: It's Niche market. “Global market” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An online shop sells in 5 countries; each gives £400 sales. Total, in £?
items.why: The real figure is 2000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
